name: Describe GitHub Security suites, features & ecosystem
tagline: Three suites, one platform: Secret Protection, Code Security, and Supply Chain Security — where each runs in the SDLC, what's free on public repos vs. what you buy, and how Security Overview ties it all together.
overview: The framing domain for GH-500 (15–20%). It tests whether you can *place* every GitHub security feature: which of the three suites owns it, where in the software development lifecycle it acts (prevention-first at the keyboard vs. gate-based in CI/PR), and whether it's free on public repositories or needs a paid Advanced Security product. The July-2026 refresh is a currency trap — GitHub Advanced Security is now sold as two standalone products, **GitHub Secret Protection** and **GitHub Code Security**, and the exam uses the current suite names: Secret Protection (formerly secret scanning), Supply Chain Security (formerly Dependabot/Dependency Review), and Code Security (formerly Code Scanning with CodeQL). You need Security Overview as the cross-cutting reporting surface, security campaigns as the scale-remediation play, and the end-to-end secure SDLC story.
objectives: Contrast the three suites — Secret Protection, Code Security, and Supply Chain Security — and name which feature lives in which, using current July-2026 names (with legacy names mapped once).
Differentiate feature availability for public repositories vs. private/enterprise, and know that GHAS is now two standalone products (Secret Protection, Code Security) you buy separately.
Explain what Security Overview provides — detection/remediation/prevention trends, risk and coverage views, and the free secret risk assessment — and at which levels it operates.
Compare prevention-first controls (push protection at commit/push) against gate-based controls (dependency review and code scanning on pull requests) and place each across the secure SDLC.
Explain security campaigns, delegated bypass, and the developer/security/admin division of responsibility for alerts and remediation.

lessons.title: The three suites and how they relate
lessons.objective: Name the three GitHub security suites, place every headline feature in the right one, and use the current July-2026 names.
lessons.body:
## One platform, three suites

GitHub groups its security capabilities into **three suites**. On GH-500 you must be able to hand a feature to the right one:

- **Secret Protection** — stops credentials leaking. Owns **secret scanning** (alerts), **push protection**, **Copilot secret scanning**, **custom patterns**, and **delegated bypass**.
- **Code Security** — finds vulnerabilities and weaknesses in *your own* code. Owns **code scanning** (with **CodeQL**), the **CodeQL CLI**, **Copilot Autofix**, **dependency review**, and **custom auto-triage rules for Dependabot**.
- **Supply Chain Security** — manages risk from your *dependencies*. Covers the **dependency graph**, **Dependabot alerts**, **Dependabot security & version updates**, **SBOM export**, and the **GitHub Advisory Database**.

> **Exam tip:** the single most common D1 distractor is putting a feature under the wrong suite. Anchor on the *object* each suite protects: **secrets** (Secret Protection), **your code** (Code Security), **your dependencies** (Supply Chain Security). Note the deliberate overlap — **dependency review** and **custom auto-triage rules** are supply-chain *tasks* but ship inside the **Code Security** product; and **security campaigns** and **Security Overview** appear in *both* paid products.

## The July-2026 rename (currency trap)

The exam refreshed in July 2026 and now uses **current** names as primary, with the old name as a parenthetical. Teach the new name; know the old one only to map it:

| Current suite / feature | Formerly |
|---|---|
| **Secret Protection** | secret scanning |
| **Supply Chain Security** | Dependabot / Dependency Review |
| **Code Security** | Code Scanning with CodeQL |

If an answer option leans on a legacy name as the *product* ("buy secret scanning", "the Dependabot suite"), treat it with suspicion — the current product names are **GitHub Secret Protection** and **GitHub Code Security**.

## GHAS is now two products, not one bundle

The biggest structural change: **"GitHub has two standalone Advanced Security products"** — **GitHub Secret Protection** and **GitHub Code Security** — that you purchase *separately*. "GitHub Advanced Security" (GHAS) survives as an umbrella term, but there is no longer a single SKU that turns everything on. To buy either product you must be on a **GitHub Team or GitHub Enterprise** plan.

Crucially, the **Supply Chain Security** basics are **not** one of those paid products — the **dependency graph, Dependabot alerts, and Dependabot updates are included in all plans**. Only the *gate-based* and *scale* supply-chain features (**dependency review**, **custom auto-triage rules**) sit inside the paid **Code Security** product.

## Why the exam cares

D1 is the map the rest of the exam is drawn on. If you can instantly say *"that's Code Security, it runs on the PR, it's free on public repos but paid on private,"* the scenario questions in D2–D6 fall out easily. Get the suite-ownership and the free-vs-paid split wrong here and you'll compound the error everywhere else.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Push protection and custom secret patterns belong to which suite?

options.text: Secret Protection
Code Security
Supply Chain Security
Security Overview

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Both stop **credentials** leaking, so they sit in **Secret Protection** alongside secret scanning alerts, Copilot secret scanning, and delegated bypass. Code Security owns code scanning/CodeQL; Supply Chain Security owns dependencies; Security Overview is a reporting surface, not a suite that owns detection features.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A teammate says 'dependency review is part of the free Supply Chain Security basics, like the dependency graph.' What's the correction?

options.text: Dependency review is a supply-chain task but ships inside the paid Code Security product; the free basics are the dependency graph, Dependabot alerts, and Dependabot updates
Dependency review is part of Secret Protection, not Supply Chain Security
Nothing — dependency review is free on all plans just like the dependency graph
Dependency review no longer exists after the July-2026 rename

knowledgeCheck.explanation: GitHub lists **dependency graph, Dependabot alerts, and Dependabot updates** as **included in all plans**, but **dependency review** (the pre-merge gate) is a **Code Security** feature you pay for. It's not a Secret Protection feature, it isn't free, and it very much still exists — it's just packaged under Code Security.

knowledgeCheck.stem: After July 2026, how is GitHub Advanced Security packaged?

options.text: As two standalone products — GitHub Secret Protection and GitHub Code Security — purchased separately, on a Team or Enterprise plan
As a single GHAS bundle SKU that enables every security feature at once
As three paid products, one per suite, including a paid Supply Chain Security product
As a free add-on automatically enabled for every repository

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The current model is **two standalone products** — **GitHub Secret Protection** and **GitHub Code Security** — bought separately (Team or Enterprise required). There is no single all-in bundle SKU anymore, there is no separate *paid* Supply Chain Security product (its basics are free on all plans), and Advanced Security is not free-by-default on every repo.

lessons.title: Public repos vs. enterprise: what's free and what you buy
lessons.objective: Decide, for any feature, whether it's free (and where) or needs a paid Advanced Security product — the availability question the exam loves.
lessons.body:
## The one distinction that unlocks the domain

GitHub's business model for security is simple to state and heavily tested: **security features that are paid on private repositories are free on public repositories.** Open source gets the tooling at no cost; you pay to run the same tooling on private/internal code.

### Free on public repositories (no purchase)

- **Code scanning** (including CodeQL) and the **CodeQL CLI**
- **Secret scanning** alerts and **push protection**
- **Copilot Autofix**

On a public repo you can turn these on without buying anything.

### Free on *all* plans and repositories (public and private)

The Supply Chain Security basics are included everywhere — you don't need a public repo or a paid product:

- **Dependency graph**
- **Dependabot alerts** (and **Dependabot malware alerts** on supported versions)
- **Dependabot security & version updates**
- **SBOM export**, **GitHub Advisory Database**, **security policies**, and **repository rulesets**

### Requires a paid Advanced Security product (mainly for private/internal)

- **GitHub Secret Protection** → Copilot secret scanning, **custom patterns**, **delegated bypass** for push protection, plus secret scanning/push protection on **private** repos.
- **GitHub Code Security** → **dependency review**, **custom auto-triage rules for Dependabot**, plus code scanning/CodeQL on **private** repos.
- Both paid products unlock the richer **Security Overview** views and **security campaigns**.

> **Exam tip:** watch for the trap that assumes *everything* is free, or that assumes you need to *buy* Dependabot. The clean rule: **detection basics for dependencies are free everywhere; deep code/secret analysis is free on public and paid on private; the advanced controls (custom patterns, delegated bypass, dependency review, auto-triage) are paid.**

## Cloud vs. Server (a light touch here, heavier in D6)

Availability also differs between **GitHub Enterprise Cloud** and **GitHub Enterprise Server** — some features are Cloud-only or arrive on Server later. D1 only needs you to know the *dimension* exists (public-vs-enterprise and Cloud-vs-Server both gate features); D6 drills the specifics.

## A worked example

A startup open-sources a library and keeps its billing service private.

- On the **public library**: enable code scanning, secret scanning, and push protection — **free**.
- On the **private billing service**: the dependency graph and Dependabot alerts work **free**, but to run code scanning and secret scanning there, and to add custom patterns or a dependency-review gate, they must buy **GitHub Code Security** and/or **GitHub Secret Protection**.

That single example is the shape of a dozen possible exam questions.

knowledgeCheck.stem: On a public repository, which of these can you enable without purchasing anything?

options.text: Code scanning, secret scanning, and push protection
Only the dependency graph — everything else is paid
Nothing — all security features require a paid product
Only features bought as part of GitHub Code Security

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Code scanning, secret scanning, and push protection are **free on public repositories**. The dependency graph is also free, but it's not the *only* free thing; and it's false that everything requires payment — the whole point is that public repos get the paid features for free.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A team runs a PRIVATE repo and wants Dependabot alerts plus a pre-merge dependency-review gate. What must they buy, if anything?

options.text: Nothing for Dependabot alerts (free on all plans), but GitHub Code Security for dependency review
GitHub Secret Protection for both
Nothing — both are free on private repos
A single GitHub Advanced Security bundle that includes both

knowledgeCheck.explanation: **Dependabot alerts** are included in **all plans**, so they cost nothing even on a private repo. **Dependency review** is a **Code Security** feature, so the gate requires purchasing **GitHub Code Security**. Secret Protection is the wrong product, dependency review isn't free on private repos, and there's no single all-in bundle SKU anymore.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Which capability requires a paid Advanced Security product even on a public repository?

options.text: Custom secret patterns and delegated bypass for push protection
Basic secret scanning alerts
Push protection for individual pushes
The dependency graph

knowledgeCheck.explanation: **Custom patterns** and **delegated bypass** are advanced **Secret Protection** features that need the paid product regardless of repo visibility. Basic secret scanning, push protection, and the dependency graph are all available for free on public repositories.

lessons.title: Security Overview: the cross-cutting reporting surface
lessons.objective: Explain what Security Overview shows, at which levels, and where the free secret risk assessment fits.
lessons.body:
## The one place to see everything

Each suite raises its own alerts, but **Security Overview** is the cross-cutting surface that **rolls them up** so an organization can "gain insights into the overall security landscape" and "identify repositories that require intervention." It answers *where is our risk, how well are we covered, and are we trending better?* — across secret scanning, Dependabot, and code scanning together.

## The views you must recognise

- **Overview dashboard** — trends in **Detection, Remediation, and Prevention** of security alerts over time.
- **Risk view** — where your risk sits, from "specific vulnerable dependencies, code weaknesses, or leaked secrets."
- **Coverage view** — adoption of security features across repositories (who has what turned on).
- **Enablement** — how quickly teams are adopting features.
- **Alert-specific views** — Dependabot (prioritise critical vulnerabilities), Secret Scanning (which secret types are blocked by push protection), and CodeQL pull-request impact.
- **Security campaigns** — coordinate and measure targeted remediation (covered in the next lesson).

## Levels

Security Overview operates at three levels: **repository** (default branches), **organization**, and **enterprise**. The higher you go, the more it's about coverage and governance across many repos.

## Free vs. premium

A **GitHub Team** organization gets a **free secret risk assessment** — "a free report to scan the code in the organization for leaked secrets" — to gauge exposure before buying anything. Organizations that purchase **GitHub Secret Protection or GitHub Code Security** unlock the **additional-insight views** beyond that basic assessment.

> **Exam tip:** Security Overview is **reporting and coverage**, not a place that *detects* anything on its own — the suites detect; Overview aggregates. If an option claims Security Overview "scans code" or "blocks pushes," it's conflating the reporting surface with the suites. The one thing it *does* offer standalone-free is the **secret risk assessment**.

## Why the exam cares

D1's task list explicitly includes "Explain features and benefits of the Security Overview." Expect a question that hands you a goal — *"see which repos haven't enabled code scanning"* (Coverage), *"show leaked-secret exposure before we buy"* (free secret risk assessment), *"track whether remediation is improving"* (Overview dashboard trends) — and asks which view or surface answers it.

knowledgeCheck.stem: An org owner wants to see which repositories have NOT yet enabled code scanning. Which Security Overview view fits?

options.text: The Coverage view (feature adoption across repositories)
The Risk view
The Overview dashboard's remediation trend
The free secret risk assessment

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Feature adoption — who has what turned on — is the **Coverage view**. The Risk view is about where vulnerabilities/secrets live, the remediation trend is about how fast alerts are being closed, and the secret risk assessment specifically measures leaked-secret exposure, not code-scanning coverage.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Which is a fair description of Security Overview?

options.text: A reporting and coverage surface that aggregates alerts from the suites across repo, org, and enterprise levels
A scanning engine that detects secrets and vulnerabilities itself
A CI system that blocks pull requests
A billing page for Advanced Security products

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Security Overview **aggregates and reports** — the suites do the detecting and the CI gates do the blocking. It runs at repository, organization, and enterprise levels. It isn't itself a scanner, a CI gate, or a billing surface.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A GitHub Team org hasn't bought any Advanced Security product yet but wants a sense of its leaked-secret exposure. What can it use?

options.text: The free secret risk assessment in Security Overview
Nothing — every Security Overview view requires a paid product
The full Risk view across all alert types, free
Custom secret patterns, which are free on Team plans

knowledgeCheck.explanation: GitHub Team orgs get a **free secret risk assessment** — a report scanning the org's code for leaked secrets — without buying anything. The *additional-insight* views do require a paid product (so 'nothing is free' and 'the full Risk view is free' are both wrong), and custom patterns are a paid Secret Protection feature.

lessons.title: Prevention-first vs. gate-based: the secure SDLC end to end
lessons.objective: Place each control across the secure SDLC and distinguish prevention-first (push-time) from gate-based (PR/CI-time) strategies, including campaigns for backlog.
lessons.body:
## Two philosophies, one lifecycle

GitHub's security controls act at different moments, and D1 asks you to compare two strategies:

- **Prevention-first** — stop the problem *at the source*, at the developer's keyboard. The flagship is **push protection**: rather than "alerting you to credential leaks *after the fact*," it **blocks pushes that contain secrets *before* they reach your repository.** Nothing bad ever lands. This is the "shift-left" ideal — cheapest possible fix, because there's nothing to clean up.
- **Gate-based** — let work proceed, then **inspect at a checkpoint** (usually the pull request or a CI run) and block or flag if it fails. **Dependency review** (flagging risky dependency changes on the PR) and **code scanning on pull requests** are gates: the code exists, and the gate decides whether it may advance.

Neither is "better" — they compose. Prevention-first catches what it can at push time; gates catch what slips through, at merge time; and detection/alerting (secret scanning alerts, Dependabot alerts) provides the backstop for anything already in the codebase.

## Mapping controls across the SDLC

| Stage | Control | Suite | Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Write / commit / **push** | **Push protection** | Secret Protection | Prevention-first |
| Open **pull request** | **Dependency review** | Code Security | Gate-based |
| PR / **CI run** | **Code scanning (CodeQL)** | Code Security | Gate-based |
| Any time (backstop) | **Secret scanning alerts**, **Dependabot alerts** | Secret Protection / Supply Chain | Detection |
| Backlog burn-down | **Security campaigns** | Code Security / Secret Protection | Coordinated remediation |

> **Exam tip:** the D1 task statement is literally "Compare prevention-first approaches vs. gate-based security strategies." Map the verb: **block at push = prevention-first**; **check on the PR/CI = gate-based**; **alert on existing code = detection**. And note the interplay the guide calls out between **Secret Protection and Code Security** — one guards secrets at the source, the other analyses code quality/vulnerabilities, and together they cover the developer inner loop end to end.

## Campaigns: fixing what already got in

Prevention and gates handle *new* work; they don't clear a **backlog of existing alerts**. That's what **security campaigns** are for — "fix security alerts at scale" by grouping related alerts and collaborating with developers to "burn down your security backlog." A **code campaign** even auto-triggers **Copilot Autofix** to suggest resolutions; a **secret campaign** does not. Campaigns are a *response/remediation* strategy, not a detection one — remember that placement.

## Putting it together: one repo, end to end

1. A developer tries to push an AWS key → **push protection blocks it** (prevention-first). 
2. They open a PR bumping a library → **dependency review flags** that the new version has a known vuln (gate). 
3. CI runs **CodeQL code scanning** → a SQL-injection alert appears, with **Copilot Autofix** offering a patch (gate + remediation). 
4. Meanwhile **Dependabot** and **secret scanning alerts** watch the existing codebase (detection). 
5. A quarter's worth of accumulated code-scanning alerts gets cleared through a **security campaign** (scale remediation). 
6. Throughout, **Security Overview** shows the org whether detection, remediation, and prevention are trending the right way.

That is the secure SDLC the exam wants you to narrate — every suite, at the right moment, under the right strategy.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Which control is the clearest example of a PREVENTION-FIRST strategy?

options.text: Push protection blocking a secret before it reaches the repository
Code scanning flagging a vulnerability on the pull request
Dependency review reporting a risky dependency change at merge time
A Dependabot alert on a dependency already in the codebase

knowledgeCheck.explanation: **Push protection** stops the problem *at the source* — the secret never lands — which is the definition of prevention-first. Code scanning and dependency review are **gate-based** (they check at the PR/CI checkpoint), and a Dependabot alert on existing code is **detection**, not prevention.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You have hundreds of pre-existing code scanning alerts spread across many repos and need to drive them down collaboratively. Which GitHub capability is designed for that?

options.text: A security campaign
Push protection
The dependency graph
A secret risk assessment

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Clearing a **backlog of existing alerts at scale** is exactly what a **security campaign** does — group related alerts, assign a point of contact, and burn them down (with Copilot Autofix on code campaigns). Push protection prevents *new* secrets, the dependency graph inventories dependencies, and a secret risk assessment reports leaked-secret exposure — none drives bulk remediation.

knowledgeCheck.stem: How do Secret Protection and Code Security 'interplay' across the developer inner loop, per the exam's framing?

options.text: Secret Protection guards credentials at the source (e.g. push protection) while Code Security analyses the code itself for vulnerabilities; together they cover commit-to-merge
They are the same product under two names after the July-2026 rename
Code Security handles secrets and Secret Protection handles code — the names are reversed
Only one can be enabled per repository, so they never operate together

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The two are complementary: **Secret Protection** keeps secrets out (prevention at push), **Code Security** finds weaknesses in the code (scanning, often at the PR/CI gate). They're distinct **standalone products** — not the same thing, not name-swapped — and can absolutely run on the same repo at once.

questions.stem: Which GitHub security suite owns code scanning with CodeQL?

options.text: Code Security
Secret Protection
Supply Chain Security
Security Overview

questions.explanation: **Code Security** (formerly 'Code Scanning with CodeQL') owns code scanning, the CodeQL CLI, and Copilot Autofix. Secret Protection owns secrets, Supply Chain Security owns dependencies, and Security Overview is a reporting surface, not a suite.

questions.stem: 'Secret Protection' is the current product name for what was formerly called:

options.text: Secret scanning
Dependabot
Code scanning
Dependency review

questions.explanation: The July-2026 refresh renamed **secret scanning** to **Secret Protection** as the product/suite name. Dependabot/Dependency Review became **Supply Chain Security**, and code scanning with CodeQL became **Code Security** — those are the other renames, not this one.

questions.stem: After the July-2026 change, which statement about buying GitHub Advanced Security is correct?

options.text: You buy two standalone products — GitHub Secret Protection and GitHub Code Security — separately
You buy a single GHAS bundle that enables all security features
You buy a separate paid Supply Chain Security product for the dependency graph
Advanced Security is free on all private repositories

questions.explanation: GitHub now sells **two standalone products** — **Secret Protection** and **Code Security** — purchased separately. There's no single all-in bundle SKU; there's no paid Supply Chain Security product (its basics are free on all plans); and Advanced Security features are paid on private repos, not free.

questions.stem: Which capabilities are free on public repositories without purchasing an Advanced Security product?

options.text: Code scanning, secret scanning, and push protection
Custom secret patterns and delegated bypass
Dependency review and custom auto-triage rules
Nothing — public repos still require payment

questions.explanation: **Code scanning, secret scanning, and push protection** are free on public repositories. Custom patterns, delegated bypass, dependency review, and custom auto-triage are **paid** features in Secret Protection or Code Security, and it's false that public repos require payment for the core scanners.

questions.stem: Which supply-chain features are included in ALL GitHub plans, even on private repositories, at no extra cost?

options.text: Dependency graph, Dependabot alerts, and Dependabot updates
Dependency review and dependency graph
Custom auto-triage rules for Dependabot
Security campaigns for supply-chain alerts

questions.explanation: GitHub lists **dependency graph, Dependabot alerts, and Dependabot updates** as included in all plans. **Dependency review** and **custom auto-triage rules** are **Code Security** (paid) features, and **security campaigns** require a paid product too.

questions.stem: A repository needs secret scanning and push protection on a PRIVATE codebase. What's required?

options.text: Purchase GitHub Secret Protection
Purchase GitHub Code Security
Nothing — these are free on all repositories
Make the repository public first; there is no paid option

questions.explanation: On private repos, secret scanning and push protection require **GitHub Secret Protection**. Code Security is the wrong product (it covers code scanning/dependency review), they aren't free on private repos, and you don't have to make the repo public — buying Secret Protection is the intended path.

questions.stem: Which control is prevention-first rather than gate-based?

options.text: Push protection blocking a secret before it reaches the repository
Dependency review on a pull request
Code scanning running in CI on a pull request
A Dependabot alert raised on an existing dependency

questions.explanation: **Push protection** stops the secret *at the source* before it lands — prevention-first. Dependency review and PR-time code scanning are **gates** (checkpoints at merge/CI), and a Dependabot alert on existing code is **detection**.

questions.stem: Which is the best description of a gate-based security control?

options.text: Dependency review flagging a risky dependency change on a pull request before merge
Push protection rejecting a commit that contains a token
A secret risk assessment report
The dependency graph listing installed packages

questions.explanation: A **gate** checks at a checkpoint and can block advancement — **dependency review on the PR** is exactly that. Push protection is prevention-first (blocks at push, not at a review gate), a risk assessment is reporting, and the dependency graph is an inventory.

questions.stem: What does Security Overview primarily provide?

options.text: Cross-cutting reporting on detection, remediation, prevention, risk, and coverage across repositories
A scanning engine that finds vulnerabilities itself
A pull-request merge gate
Automated pull requests to update dependencies

questions.explanation: Security Overview **aggregates and reports** — trends, risk, and coverage — across the suites. It doesn't scan (the suites do), it isn't a merge gate (dependency review/code scanning are), and it doesn't open update PRs (that's Dependabot updates).

questions.stem: Which Security Overview capability is available to a GitHub Team organization WITHOUT purchasing an Advanced Security product?

options.text: A free secret risk assessment of the organization's code
The full Risk view across all alert types
Security campaigns
Custom auto-triage rules for Dependabot

questions.explanation: Team orgs get a **free secret risk assessment** to gauge leaked-secret exposure. The richer views (full Risk view), **security campaigns**, and **custom auto-triage rules** all require a paid product.

questions.stem: What problem do security campaigns solve?

options.text: Coordinated remediation of existing alerts at scale — burning down a security backlog with developers
Detecting new secrets as they are pushed
Generating the SBOM for a repository
Enforcing branch protection rules

questions.explanation: Security campaigns group **existing** alerts and drive **collaborative remediation at scale** (backlog burn-down). Detection of new secrets is push protection/secret scanning, SBOMs come from the dependency graph, and branch protection is a repository ruleset — none is a campaign's job.

questions.stem: In a security campaign, which alert type has Copilot Autofix automatically triggered to suggest a resolution?

options.text: Code scanning alerts (a 'code campaign')
Secret scanning alerts (a 'secret campaign')
Dependabot version-update pull requests
Every alert type in any campaign

questions.explanation: For **code campaigns**, **Copilot Autofix is automatically triggered** to suggest a fix. **Secret campaigns** have no such automatic remediation. Version-update PRs aren't campaign alerts, and autofix isn't attached to every alert type.

questions.stem: What is 'delegated bypass' for push protection?

options.text: A control where designated actors can bypass push protection and review/approve bypass requests from others, rather than anyone with write access bypassing freely
A feature that permanently disables push protection for a repository
A way to auto-merge pull requests that contain secrets
The default behavior on all public repositories

questions.explanation: **Delegated bypass** routes bypasses through **designated approvers** who can grant or deny requests — tightening control versus the default where anyone with write access can bypass with a reason. It doesn't disable push protection, doesn't auto-merge secret-bearing PRs, and isn't the default (it's a paid Secret Protection control you configure).

questions.stem: By default (without delegated bypass configured), who can bypass push protection, and what happens when they do?

options.text: Anyone with write access can bypass by giving a reason; GitHub records the event, may open an alert, and notifies admins/security managers
No one can ever bypass push protection once it is enabled
Only enterprise owners can bypass, and no record is kept
Anyone can bypass silently with no alert or audit trail

questions.explanation: By default **anyone with write access** can bypass by specifying a reason; GitHub then **logs the event, creates an alert (open or closed depending on the reason), and notifies repository admins and security managers**. Bypass is possible (so 'no one can' is wrong), it isn't enterprise-owner-only, and it is never silent — the audit trail is the point.

questions.stem: In what format does GitHub export a repository's SBOM?

options.text: SPDX (the industry-standard format, version 2.3)
CycloneDX only
A proprietary GitHub-only binary format
SARIF

questions.explanation: GitHub exports the SBOM in the **industry-standard SPDX** format (v2.3), built from the dependency graph. It's not a proprietary format, and **SARIF** is the format for *code scanning* results, not SBOMs.

questions.stem: Which metric does GitHub surface to help prioritize which Dependabot alerts are most likely to be exploited?

options.text: EPSS (Exploit Prediction Scoring System) percentage
The SPDX license identifier
The number of GitHub stars on the dependency
The size of the package in kilobytes

questions.explanation: **EPSS**, from FIRST, gives a probability (0–1) that a vulnerability will be exploited; GitHub exposes an `epss_percentage` metric/filter so teams can prioritize likely-exploited alerts. License IDs, stars, and package size aren't exploit-likelihood signals.

questions.stem: A 'security configuration' in GitHub is best described as:

options.text: A collection of security-feature enablement settings you can apply to repositories across an organization
A single on/off switch for all of Advanced Security
A CodeQL query pack
A billing plan for private repositories

questions.explanation: A **security configuration** is a reusable **bundle of enablement settings** (which features are on) applied to repos; the **GitHub-recommended** one turns on Code Security and Secret Protection features. It's not a single global switch, not a CodeQL query pack, and not a billing plan.

questions.stem: Which pairing of task → suite/surface is correct?

options.text: Block a hardcoded token at push time → Secret Protection (push protection)
Find a SQL-injection flaw in your own code → Supply Chain Security
Inventory your dependencies as an SBOM → Secret Protection
See which repos enabled code scanning → a Dependabot alert

questions.explanation: Blocking a token at push is **Secret Protection / push protection**. The others are mis-mapped: a SQL-injection flaw in your code is **Code Security**, the SBOM is **Supply Chain Security** (dependency graph), and feature-adoption reporting is **Security Overview's Coverage view** — not a Dependabot alert.

questions.stem: Which statements about the July-2026 GitHub security suites are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: GitHub Secret Protection and GitHub Code Security are two standalone products bought separately
The dependency graph, Dependabot alerts, and Dependabot updates are free on all plans
Security Overview aggregates alerts across the suites and is not itself a scanner
'Supply Chain Security' is a paid standalone product you must purchase like the other two suites

questions.explanation: A, B, and C are all correct: the two paid products are Secret Protection and Code Security; the dependency-graph/Dependabot basics are free on all plans; and Security Overview reports rather than scans. D is wrong — Supply Chain Security is a *conceptual* suite whose basics are free; the only supply-chain features you *pay* for (dependency review, custom auto-triage) live inside the **Code Security** product, not a separate Supply Chain Security SKU.

questions.stem: On the GH-500 study guide, 'Supply Chain Security' is the current umbrella name for features formerly known as:

options.text: Dependabot / Dependency Review
Secret scanning
Code Scanning with CodeQL
Copilot Autofix

questions.explanation: The study guide names the domain **'supply chain security (formerly Dependabot/Dependency Review)'**. Secret scanning became Secret Protection, Code Scanning with CodeQL became Code Security, and Copilot Autofix is a Code Security feature — none of those is the supply-chain rename.

questions.stem: An architect claims: 'Because we bought GitHub Code Security, our private repos are fully covered — including secret scanning and push protection.' What's the flaw?

options.text: Code Security covers code scanning and dependency review, but secret scanning and push protection on private repos need GitHub Secret Protection — a separate product
Nothing — Code Security includes secret scanning and push protection
Code Security only works on public repos, so private repos get nothing
Secret scanning is free on private repos, so no purchase was ever needed

questions.explanation: The two products are **separate**: **Code Security** unlocks code scanning, CodeQL, and dependency review; **secret scanning and push protection** on private repos require **Secret Protection**. So buying one doesn't cover the other. Code Security does work on private repos (that's what you pay for), and secret scanning is *not* free on private repos.

scenarios.title: Adopt all three suites on one repository
scenarios.tagline: Place each control in the right suite, at the right SDLC moment.
scenarios.context: You own a private service and want end-to-end coverage: keep secrets out, catch vulnerable dependencies before merge, and find flaws in your own code. Walk the secure SDLC and pick the right control — and the right suite — at each step, minding the current names and what needs buying.

steps.prompt: First priority: make it impossible to commit a hardcoded credential in the first place. What do you turn on?

choices.text: Push protection (Secret Protection) — it blocks the push before the secret lands
choices.rationale: Prevention-first: push protection stops the secret at the source, the cheapest possible fix. It's a Secret Protection feature.

choices.text: Secret scanning alerts only — let it flag secrets after they're pushed
choices.rationale: Detection is a useful backstop, but it fires *after* the leak; the ask was to prevent the commit, which is push protection's job.

choices.text: Code scanning (CodeQL) — it will catch the secret as a code vulnerability
choices.rationale: Code scanning analyses code for vulnerabilities/weaknesses, not for hardcoded credentials — wrong suite for secrets.

steps.prompt: Next: stop a pull request that pulls in a dependency with a known vulnerability, before it merges. What do you add?

choices.text: Dependency review (Code Security) as a pre-merge gate on the PR
choices.rationale: Dependency review is the gate-based control that flags risky dependency changes on the PR before merge. Note it ships inside the paid Code Security product.

choices.text: Rely on Dependabot alerts to notice the vulnerable dependency later
choices.rationale: Dependabot alerts (free) will detect it, but *after* it's merged — the step specifically wants a pre-merge gate, which is dependency review.

choices.text: Push protection, so the risky dependency is blocked at push
choices.rationale: Push protection is about secrets, not dependency risk — wrong tool and wrong suite for this checkpoint.

steps.prompt: Now find security flaws in your OWN source (e.g. SQL injection), ideally with a suggested fix. What do you enable?

choices.text: Code scanning with CodeQL (Code Security), which can surface Copilot Autofix suggestions
choices.rationale: Code Security / CodeQL analyses your code for vulnerabilities and can attach Copilot Autofix — exactly this need.

choices.text: The dependency graph, to inventory what your code imports
choices.rationale: The dependency graph inventories *dependencies*, not flaws in your own code — wrong suite entirely.

choices.text: A secret risk assessment to scan for leaked secrets
choices.rationale: That reports leaked-secret exposure, not code vulnerabilities like SQL injection.

steps.prompt: This is a private repo. Which purchase(s) does this end-to-end setup actually require?

choices.text: GitHub Secret Protection (for push protection on private) and GitHub Code Security (for code scanning + dependency review) — bought separately
choices.rationale: The two products are standalone: Secret Protection covers the push-protection piece on private repos; Code Security covers code scanning and dependency review. Dependabot basics stay free.

choices.text: A single GitHub Advanced Security bundle that turns everything on
choices.rationale: There's no all-in bundle SKU anymore — GHAS is two standalone products.

choices.text: Only GitHub Code Security — it includes secret scanning and push protection too
choices.rationale: Code Security doesn't include the Secret Protection features; secret scanning/push protection on private repos need Secret Protection.

scenarios.title: Choose the strategy and the reporting surface
scenarios.tagline: Prevention-first vs. gate-based vs. remediation — and where Security Overview fits.
scenarios.context: You're the security lead for an org with dozens of repos. Leadership wants fewer leaked secrets, a way to clear a large existing alert backlog, and visibility into whether things are improving. Pick the right strategy and surface at each turn.

steps.prompt: Goal: drive leaked secrets toward zero by stopping them at the source across the org. Which strategy leads?

choices.text: A prevention-first approach centred on push protection
choices.rationale: Stopping secrets before they land is prevention-first; push protection is the flagship control for it.

choices.text: A gate-based approach that only checks secrets on the pull request
choices.rationale: A PR gate helps, but it's later and leakier than blocking at push; prevention-first is the stronger lead for secrets at the source.

choices.text: Rely solely on after-the-fact secret scanning alerts
choices.rationale: Detection-only means the secret already leaked; it doesn't 'stop them at the source' as asked.

steps.prompt: There's a backlog of hundreds of existing code scanning alerts across many repos. How do you drive them down collaboratively?

choices.text: Run a security campaign — group the alerts, assign a point of contact, and let Copilot Autofix suggest fixes
choices.rationale: Campaigns are purpose-built for coordinated backlog burn-down at scale, and code campaigns auto-trigger Copilot Autofix.

choices.text: Turn on more push protection to prevent new alerts
choices.rationale: Prevention handles *new* issues; it does nothing for the existing backlog the step is about.

choices.text: Ask each team to work through their alerts ad hoc, untracked
choices.rationale: Work might happen, but with no coordination, ownership, or measurement — exactly the gaps a campaign closes.

steps.prompt: Leadership wants to know whether detection, remediation, and prevention are trending better month over month. Where do you look?

choices.text: Security Overview — its dashboard shows detection/remediation/prevention trends across repos
choices.rationale: Security Overview is the cross-cutting reporting surface for exactly these trends at org/enterprise scope.

choices.text: Each repository's individual alert list, added up by hand
choices.rationale: You could reconstruct some of it manually, but that's what Security Overview aggregates for you — the intended surface.

choices.text: The dependency graph for each repo
choices.rationale: The dependency graph inventories packages; it doesn't report security trends across the org.

labs.title: Map a real repo's features to the three suites
labs.goal: Turn the suite model from theory into a picture of one real repository — and see the free-vs-paid line for yourself.
labs.steps:
## What you'll do

Use any repository you can open in a browser (a public one works and needs no purchase).

1. Open the repo and go to the **Security** tab. Note the entry points you see (e.g. **Secret scanning alerts**, **Dependabot alerts / Dependency graph**, **Code scanning alerts**).
2. Open **Settings → (Advanced) Security** (or **Code security** on newer UIs). List every toggle you can see and, next to each, write which **suite** it belongs to — Secret Protection, Code Security, or Supply Chain Security.
3. For each toggle, mark **F** if it's free here (public repo) and **$** if the UI says it needs a paid product / is greyed out on private repos. Confirm your marks against `r-ghas-overview`'s free-vs-paid lists.
4. Find one **prevention-first** control (push protection) and one **gate-based** control (dependency review / code scanning on PRs) and note where in the SDLC each acts.
5. On the **Insights → Dependency graph**, click **Export SBOM** and confirm the format (SPDX). That single click proves the Supply Chain Security basics are free.

## Why it matters

D1 is entirely about placing features. Doing it on a live repo — suite, free-vs-paid, prevention-vs-gate — burns the map in far better than a table does.
checklist: Every visible security toggle is labelled with its suite
Each feature marked F (free here) or $ (paid), checked against the docs
One prevention-first and one gate-based control identified with its SDLC stage
SBOM exported and its format (SPDX) confirmed

labs.title: Read Security Overview and place the strategies
labs.goal: See what Security Overview reports vs. what the suites do, and articulate prevention-first vs. gate-based vs. remediation.
labs.steps:
## Steps

Use an organization you have access to (a free personal org works for the reporting basics; some views need a paid product — note which are greyed out).

1. Open the org's **Security** tab → **Overview**. Identify the **Overview dashboard** (Detection / Remediation / Prevention trends), the **Risk** view, and the **Coverage** view. Write one sentence on what each answers.
2. Find the **Coverage** view and note which repos have or haven't enabled a given feature — this is the 'who's covered' question the exam asks.
3. If your org is on **Team**, locate the **free secret risk assessment** and run it (or note where it would run). Confirm it needs *no* purchase.
4. Now categorise three controls in writing: **push protection** (prevention-first), **dependency review / PR code scanning** (gate-based), **security campaigns** (coordinated remediation). For each, state *when in the SDLC* it acts.
5. Note which Overview views are greyed out / marked as needing **Secret Protection** or **Code Security** — that boundary is the free-vs-paid line for reporting.

## Checkpoint

If you can point at the Coverage view for 'who enabled code scanning', the dashboard for 'are we trending better', and name push-protection/dependency-review/campaigns as prevention/gate/remediation respectively, you've internalised D1's core distinctions.
checklist: Overview dashboard, Risk view, and Coverage view each identified and described
Located where the free secret risk assessment runs (no purchase needed)
Three controls correctly labelled prevention-first / gate-based / remediation with their SDLC stage
Noted which Overview views require a paid product

resources.title: Study guide for Exam GH-500: GitHub Advanced Security
resources.annotation: The authoritative task list for D1 ('Describe GitHub Security suites, features, and ecosystem', 15–20%). Extract the exact sub-bullets — suite structure, contrast the three suites, public-vs-enterprise availability, Security Overview, secure SDLC, prevention-first vs. gate-based, campaigns — and the July-2026 rename ('formerly ...') that drives the currency trap.

resources.title: About GitHub Advanced Security
resources.annotation: The single most load-bearing page for D1: GHAS is now two standalone products (Secret Protection, Code Security) bought separately on Team/Enterprise; which features fall in each; and the free-vs-paid split — dependency graph/Dependabot alerts/updates free on all plans, code scanning/secret scanning/push protection free on public repos, custom patterns/delegated bypass/dependency review/auto-triage paid.

resources.title: GitHub security features
resources.annotation: The full feature inventory grouped by tier — 'available for all plans', 'GitHub Secret Protection', 'GitHub Code Security'. Extract which suite owns each feature and the exact availability wording, to place any feature the exam names.

resources.title: About security overview
resources.annotation: What Security Overview provides: the Overview dashboard (Detection/Remediation/Prevention trends), Risk view, Coverage view, Enablement, and alert-specific views; the three levels (repo/org/enterprise); and the free secret risk assessment for Team orgs vs. premium views under the paid products.

resources.title: About push protection
resources.annotation: The prevention-first flagship: push protection blocks pushes containing secrets 'before they reach your repository' (vs. alerting after the fact). Extract delegated bypass (designated approvers review/approve bypass requests), the default 'anyone with write access can bypass with a reason' behaviour, the audit/alert/notification trail, and on-by-default status for public user repos.

resources.title: About security campaigns
resources.annotation: Security campaigns = coordinated remediation of existing alerts at scale ('burn down your security backlog'). Extract the code-campaign vs. secret-campaign distinction, that code campaigns auto-trigger Copilot Autofix (secret campaigns don't), the named point of contact, and template/custom-filter alert selection.

resources.title: Choosing a security configuration for your repositories
resources.annotation: A security configuration is a reusable collection of feature-enablement settings applied to repos; the GitHub-recommended configuration bundles Code Security and Secret Protection features and is the fastest way to cover an org. Extract how configurations apply and that private/internal application incurs cost/licenses.

resources.title: About Dependabot alerts
resources.annotation: Dependabot alerts fire when a reviewed advisory in the GitHub Advisory Database affects a dependency in the dependency graph. Confirms these Supply Chain basics are core (free-on-all-plans per r-ghas-overview) and that malware alerts are a related capability.

resources.title: Prioritizing Dependabot alerts using metrics (EPSS)
resources.annotation: EPSS = Exploit Prediction Scoring System (from FIRST), a 0–1 probability that a vulnerability will be exploited, updated daily; GitHub exposes an epss_percentage filter to prioritize likely-exploited alerts. Extract the exact definition and how it combines with severity and patch availability.

resources.title: Exporting a software bill of materials for your repository
resources.annotation: GitHub exports the SBOM in industry-standard SPDX (v2.3) from the current dependency graph, via the UI (Insights → Dependency graph → Export SBOM) or the REST API. Contrast SPDX (SBOM) with SARIF (code scanning results).

videos.title: D1 · The three suites — Secret Protection, Code Security & Supply Chain Security
D1 · Secure SDLC — prevention-first vs. gate-based, Security Overview & campaigns
